web.app.bias_summary: Who the data over- and under-reports
web.app.blEmpty: Bottom line: no analyzed segments in this dataset yet.
web.app.blNone: Bottom line: none of the {n} analyzed blocks is a statistically significant hotspot — no street is hotter than exposure and chance explain. Raw volume is not danger.
web.app.blSummary: Bottom line: {hot} of {n} analyzed blocks are statistically significant hotspots — hotter than traffic (exposure) explains. Highest rate per rider: {list}. Raw volume is not danger.
web.app.capRate: The same segments by exposure-normalized rate. {hot} significant hotspot(s) — dashed and labeled — emerge, while the most-reported street recedes to a thin line.
web.app.capRateNone: The same segments by exposure-normalized rate. No segment is a statistically significant hotspot; the most-reported street recedes.
web.app.capReports: {n} street segments. Thicker, darker lines have more reports. The most-reported street ({peak}) dominates — but volume is exposure, not danger.
web.app.caption: Exposure-normalized hazard rates for the {n} analyzed segments (streets with reports).
web.app.captionFiltered: Exposure-normalized hazard rates — showing {shown} of {n} analyzed segments (filtered).
web.app.demo: ⚠️ Showing the Davis synthetic demo dataset — generated test data, not real reports. The method is the point: swap in real reports with no code change.
web.app.demo_synth: ⚠️ Showing the {city} synthetic demo dataset — generated test data, not real reports.
web.app.fail: Could not load published data ({msg}). Run `make publish`, then `nearmiss serve` and open /web/index.html.
web.app.faq_a1: Because volume is not danger. A busy street collects the most reports simply because the most people use it. We divide reports by exposure (how much cycling each street carries), so the map shows the rate per rider, not the raw count. A high-traffic street can have many reports and still be safer per trip than a quiet one with a few.
web.app.faq_a2: No. These are self-reported near-misses and hazards, which by definition usually leave no police report. They are an early-warning signal, not verified injuries or collision statistics, and we never present them as such.
web.app.faq_a3: It means we have no trustworthy count of how many people cycle that street, so we will not invent a denominator. Those segments are shown as uncertain and are never ranked as if we were sure. A rate without a denominator is not published.
web.app.faq_a4: A street is marked “★ Significant” only when a statistical test (Getis-Ord Gi*, with a false-discovery-rate correction) says it is hotter than exposure and chance alone would explain. Everything else carries a confidence interval so you can see how sure — or unsure — the number is.
web.app.faq_q1: Why isn’t my busy street the most dangerous one?
web.app.faq_q2: Is a near-miss the same as a crash?
web.app.faq_q4: How do I know which streets are really hotspots?
web.app.footer: Open data, Apache-2.0. Source on GitHub · chelseakr.com. Maps © OpenStreetMap contributors. Methods: docs/METHODOLOGY.md; what it can't tell you: docs/LIMITATIONS.md; limits and biases: docs/DATA-CARD.md. Every figure regenerates with make reproduce.
web.app.h1: nearmiss — where the danger actually is
web.app.hsSummary: {n} statistically significant hotspot(s) — hotter than exposure and chance explain: {list}.
web.app.hsSummaryNone: No statistically significant hotspots in this dataset (no street is hotter than exposure and chance explain).
web.app.lede: Road-hazard and near-miss rates, normalized by exposure, each with a 95% confidence interval and an n. Raw counts are report volume, not danger. The two maps below show the difference; the data table carries every finding and is the authoritative, non-visual equivalent of the maps.
web.app.legend:
• Rate /1000 — reports per 1000 units of exposure, not a raw count.
  • 95% CI — the plausible range. A wide range means uncertainty.
  • Confidence — “uncertain” marks small samples; “exposure unknown” marks segments with no denominator (never ranked as if certain).
  • Hotspot (Gi*) — “★ Significant” marks a statistically significant cluster (Getis-Ord Gi*, z > 1.96): hot beyond what exposure and chance explain.
web.app.mapNoLeaflet: The interactive maps need JavaScript and the map library. The data table below carries every finding.
web.app.map_desc: The same near-miss reports, mapped two ways on a real street map. On the left, the raw report count — what most safety maps show: the busiest street looks the most dangerous. On the right, the rate per 1000 units of exposure — which is what actually reflects danger. Watch the busiest street recede and the real hotspot emerge. Nothing is conveyed by color alone: line thickness scales with the value, and significant hotspots are dashed and labeled. Everything here is in the data table, which the maps supplement.
web.app.map_rate_t: ② Exposure-normalized rate — where the danger actually is
web.app.map_reports_t: ① Raw report count — what most maps show
web.app.print_caveat: Rates are shown with 95% confidence intervals. Segments without a trustworthy exposure denominator are shown as uncertain and are never ranked as if certain.
web.app.print_footer: nearmiss — near-miss rates, normalized by exposure · https://nearmiss.report
web.app.sort_help: Sort the table with the column buttons. Significance and confidence are stated in words, not by color.
web.app.title: nearmiss — where the danger actually is (Davis)
web.app.titleCity: nearmiss — where the danger actually is ({city})
web.app.tt_ci: 95% confidence interval: the plausible range. Wide means uncertain.
web.app.tt_hazards: What kinds of hazard were reported here (suppressed for very small samples).
web.app.tt_hot: Getis-Ord Gi* z-score; ★ Significant is a real cluster (z > 1.96, FDR-corrected).
web.app.tt_n: Number of reports on this segment (the sample size).
web.app.tt_rate: Reports per 1000 units of exposure — risk per rider, not a raw count.
web.app.tt_tier: How much to trust this segment's exposure denominator: observed (direct count), modeled (demand model), proxy (third-party activity layer), or unknown.
web.app.us_coverage_cta: This Davis page is a synthetic demo. Explore separate, nationwide US evidence from reviewed 2020–2024 fatal-crash records →
web.coverage.brief_empty: No evidence saved yet. Select a state, then add it from the inspector.
web.coverage.brief_intro: Save up to four states. The printable brief keeps the year, mode, publication status, exact release, source, and ‘counts are not risk’ limit attached.
web.coverage.brief_kicker: Carry the caveat with the number
web.coverage.can_list:
• How many fatal crashes involved each mode in a state in the selected year.
  • Which cells met the small-cell publication floor.
  • Which official release and transformation produced the counts.
web.coverage.cannot_list:
• Risk, rate, fault, causation, or a safety ranking.
  • Near misses, nonfatal crashes, counties, streets, or intersections.
  • Whether a non-published cell is zero or suppressed.
web.coverage.caption: Showing {shown} of {total} state × mode cells.
web.coverage.caveat: Counts are not risk. They do not account for population, trips, miles, or exposure. Modes can overlap in one crash, so mode counts are not additive and do not imply fault or causation.
web.coverage.compare_h: Compare two states without adding modes
web.coverage.compare_intro: Every mode keeps an independent scale because one crash may contribute to multiple rows.
web.coverage.copy_failed: Couldn’t copy automatically. Copy the address from your browser.
web.coverage.data_intro: A published count is the number of distinct fatal crashes with at least one person in that mode. A missing public cell is labeled “Not published (suppressed or zero)”—never zero.
web.coverage.filter_scope: State loads the five-year profile. Year, mode, and publication status refine the selected-year national views and ledger.
web.coverage.footer: Official fatal-crash context, published with explicit limits. nearmiss remains an exposure-normalized near-miss product; these counts are a separate national context layer.
web.coverage.inspector_empty: Select a map area, matrix cell, rank bar, plot point, or state control to inspect its six publication cells.
web.coverage.inspector_rank: {mode} rank among published numeric cells: {rank} of {total}.
web.coverage.lede: Choose a state or DC to inspect its reviewed 2020–2024 FARS profile, then refine the selected-year detail. Every value remains tied to its exact release, small-cell publication rule, and source.
web.coverage.ledger_empty: Choose a state above to show its selected-year evidence.
web.coverage.load_error: The reviewed release index or selected public artifact could not be loaded or did not pass its contract. No counts are shown.
web.coverage.map_caption: {mode}: {published} of 51 jurisdictions have numeric published counts. Geography is for orientation; counts are not exposure-normalized risk.
web.coverage.map_h: Fatal-crash burden across the United States
web.coverage.map_intro: Select a road-user mode to read the same reviewed counts geographically. Color is scaled within that mode; hatching means suppressed or zero.
web.coverage.matrix_caption: Matrix of {states} jurisdictions and six involved modes: {published} of {total} cells publish a numeric count.
web.coverage.matrix_intro: Each column has its own scale. Hatched cells are not published and are never treated as zero.
web.coverage.profile_caption: {state} fatal-crash burden by involved mode, 2020–2024, grouped by the reviewed FARS person-type coding regimes.
web.coverage.profile_empty: Choose a state above to load its five-year profile.
web.coverage.profile_error: The five-year profile could not be loaded from all five reviewed artifacts. No profile values are shown. The selected-year ledger below has a separate verification state.
web.coverage.profile_intro: Each year is kept separate. The coding seam marks two reviewed FARS person-type regimes, so values on opposite sides should not be read as one continuous series.
web.coverage.profile_loading: Loading and verifying all five annual artifacts…
web.coverage.profile_ready: {state} profile loaded from five reviewed annual artifacts.
web.coverage.proof_h: Five years, with publication status made explicit
web.coverage.proof_intro: Official source archives are pinned for 2020–2024. Only years whose reviewed public artifacts appear in the release index can be selected; unavailable years are never silently treated as zero.
web.coverage.provenance_intro: The public JSON and release index carry source, mapping, semantic-regime, small-cell publication, and accounting proof. Deployment files bind the published page back to an exact source commit.
web.coverage.rank_intro: Only numeric cells are ranked. This is burden, not exposure-normalized risk or safety.
web.coverage.rank_unpublished: {count} jurisdictions have no published numeric {mode} count (suppressed or zero)
web.coverage.regime_caution: Compare years with care. FARS person-type coding uses one reviewed semantic regime for 2020–2021 and another for 2022–2024. Each result stays pinned to its annual mapping contract, so a cross-regime change can reflect coding as well as outcomes.
web.coverage.release_note: Source bytes, the annual contract, and accounting proof are verified before any count appears.
web.coverage.result_summary_state: Showing {shown} of {total} involved-mode cells for {state} in {year}.
web.coverage.scatter_caption: {comparable} jurisdictions have both numeric counts; {excluded} remain outside the plot because one or both cells are not published.
web.coverage.scatter_empty: No jurisdictions have both counts published.
web.coverage.scatter_intro: States appear only when both cells are published. Missing values stay outside the plot.
web.coverage.selected_year_h: Mode-by-mode evidence for the selected year
web.coverage.studio_h: One evidence set, five ways to inspect it
web.coverage.studio_intro: Every view is driven by the same reviewed state-by-mode cells for the selected year. A selection in one view follows you into the others.
web.coverage.title: US evidence ledger — nearmiss
web.submit.geo_denied: Couldn't get your location — type a place instead.
web.submit.geo_filled: Location filled in. You can adjust it.
web.submit.geo_unavailable: Geolocation isn't available — type a location instead.
web.submit.need_location: a location (use my location, lat/lon, or an address)
web.submit.status_copy_manual: Select the text and copy it manually.
web.submit.status_ready: Your submission is ready below — it still needs human review before it counts.
web.submit.status_received: Thank you. Your near-miss was received and is awaiting review.
web.submit.status_send_failed: Couldn't reach the server — here is your submission to send manually instead.
